visual_checks: CRITICAL — STRUCTURE VERIFICATION: Determine whether the PDF has a tag tree that represents the logical structure of the document, not merely visual layout.
Open the PDF's tag panel or inspect its properties: does it report as a 'Tagged PDF'? A tagged PDF will have a Tags tree in the navigation pane.

Check whether the tag tree includes standard PDF tags such as ,

,

-

, ,
Verify that every visible content element (text paragraphs, headings, images, tables, lists, links) has a corresponding tag in the tag tree.
Look for content that appears visually but is not represented in the tag structure — this content would be invisible to screen readers.
Check whether the document was exported with 'Tagged PDF' enabled from its authoring application (Word, InDesign, etc.).

Inspect whether tags are meaningful and correct (e.g., headings tagged as

not

, table rows tagged as not

).
pass_conditions: PDF document properties confirm it is a Tagged PDF
A complete tag tree exists that maps to all visible content in the document
All text content is contained within appropriate semantic tags (paragraphs, headings, lists, table cells, etc.)
All images are either tagged as <Figure> with alt text or marked as artifacts for decorative images
Links are tagged as <Link> with appropriate link text
The tag tree uses standard PDF tags consistent with the PDF/UA specification
fail_conditions: PDF is not tagged at all — document properties show 'Tagged PDF: No'
PDF has a tag tree but it is incomplete, with significant content elements missing from the structure

All content is tagged as a single type (e.g., everything is

) with no semantic distinction between headings, lists, tables, and paragraphs

Tags exist but are meaningless or auto-generated incorrectly (e.g., every line is a separate

tag instead of logical paragraphs)
Visible content exists outside the tag tree and would be skipped by assistive technology
The PDF was scanned and has no tag structure — it is essentially a flat image with no underlying text layer or tags
na_conditions: Document is not a PDF (e.g., DOCX, PPTX, or XLSX files use their own structural mechanisms)
File is not a document type that supports tagging
ne_conditions: AI response could not be parsed
PDF could not be opened or its tag structure could not be inspected
Document is password-protected and its internal structure cannot be examined
common_mistakes: Printing to PDF from a browser or application instead of exporting as Tagged PDF — this produces an untagged flat PDF
Using 'Save As PDF' without enabling the Tagged PDF option in export settings
Scanning a paper document to PDF without running OCR and then adding tags
Auto-tagging a PDF in Acrobat without reviewing and correcting the generated tags
Having a tag tree that exists but does not match the actual content (leftover tags from editing)
Assuming that because a PDF has bookmarks it is also properly tagged — bookmarks and tags are separate features

examples.pass:
A PDF exported from Microsoft Word with 'Create Tagged PDF' enabled. The tag tree includes

for the title,

for section headings,

for body text, for data tables, and
examples.fail: A PDF created by printing a webpage to PDF. Document properties show 'Tagged PDF: No'. Screen readers cannot navigate by headings, tables, or any structural elements — they read raw text in an unpredictable order.
examples.partial: A PDF is tagged but the tag tree is incomplete: the first 10 pages are properly tagged from the original Word export, but 5 appended pages from a scanned document have no tags.
